records.display_name: DOB strategy to foster competition and reduce risk

payload.claim_text: In keeping with DOB's strategy to foster competition in the enforcement space as well as reduce risk exposure by having more than one qualified vendor, discussions with Seon resumed.
payload.subject: DOB vendor competition strategy

records.display_name: ACE expanded to bus stop and double-parking enforcement

payload.claim_text: Following legislative approval to monitor and ticket vehicles obstructing bus stops as well double-parked vehicles, DOB has updated the scope of work, changed the name of this project to Automated Camera Enforcement ('ACE').
payload.subject: Automated Camera Enforcement expansion

records.display_name: ABLE supports bus lanes and SBS success

payload.claim_text: Improving the enforcement of bus lane traffic restrictions is a critical element to ensuring the success of both bus lanes and Select Bus Service ('SBS').
payload.subject: bus lane enforcement

payload.claim_text: These contracts will be funded with 100 percent MTA funds. Funds for these procurements have been approved in the MTA 2020–2024 Capital Programs as well as the operating budget.
payload.subject: ABLE contract funding source

records.display_name: Zero percent M/WBE and SDVOB goals

payload.claim_text: The MTA Department of Diversity and Civil Rights (DDCR) has established a zero percent M/WBE and zero percent SDVOB goals on this contract due to the lack of subcontracting availability.
payload.subject: M/WBE and SDVOB goals

payload.claim_text: Upon the advice of the Law Department and outside counsel, all work with Seon regarding its ABLE system was suspended.
payload.subject: Hayden patent infringement lawsuit against Seon and Safe Fleet

records.display_name: DOB strategy to foster competition in ABLE

payload.claim_text: In an effort to foster competition, a Request for Information ('RFI') was issued in June 2019 seeking additional vendors capable of furnishing ABLE systems.
payload.subject: Department of Buses ABLE competition strategy

description: A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.

ACE will be implemented in May 2024 as a four-year demonstration. Activated on fourteen routes in May 2024, expand to another 400 buses and fourteen additional routes, then add ten routes per year from 2025 through 2027
Expansion of ACE program beyond bus lanes to vehicles blocking bus stops and double-parked vehicles. Cameras added to up to 1,000 buses and enforced on 28 bus routes.
Expanded scope of ABLE program to also monitor and ticket vehicles obstructing bus stops and double-parked vehicles, following legislative approval.
Expanded scope of ABLE program to include monitoring and ticketing vehicles obstructing bus stops as well as double-parked vehicles, following legislative approval

Expansion of the Automated Bus Lane Enforcement Program (ABLE), renamed ACE. Issues tickets to cars illegally parked in bus stops and bus lanes. Launched June 2024 on 14 existing ABLE routes with state-of-the-art enforcement technology. NYS Budget approved expansion to enforce bus lanes, bus stops, and illegal double parking during a four-year demonstration period.

Activated ACE on 34 routes in partnership with NYC DOT and NYC DOF. As of November 29, all routes transitioned from warning period to issuing violations. Bus lane speeds increased on average by 5%, 20% reduction in collisions, 5-10% estimated reduction in emissions. Covers 1,000 buses.

Expansion of Automated Camera Enforcement (ACE) adding 10 new bus routes on September 16, 2024 and another 10 on September 30, 2024. Total reaching 34 routes and over 1,000 ACE-equipped buses across all five boroughs.
Program issuing tickets for double parking, blocking bus stops, and driving in bus lanes via bus-mounted cameras
expand ACE to 30 additional routes and 1,000 more buses, beginning with the M2 and M4 at the end of April
Covers 39 routes with over 1,200 equipped buses serving 775,000 daily riders; includes bus lane, bus stop, and double-parking enforcement

Modification 2 to Hayden for bus stop and double-parked vehicle enforcement and up to 1,000 additional ACE systems; Modification 1 to Seon for bus stop, double-parked, bike lane, and paratransit stop enforcement on up to 1,000 ACE systems

Started as Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) in 2019, evolved into ACE. Uses cameras on buses to enforce bus lanes, bus stops, and double-parking rules. Active on 58 routes, covering about 560 miles of streets across all five boroughs.
Automated Camera Enforcement system for bus lanes, bus stops, double-parked vehicles, bike lanes, and paratransit stops
status: 60-day warning period started September 16, 2024 on 10 new bus routes; additional 10 routes starting September 30, 2024

payload.description: A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.

records.raw_text: Since its launch in June 2024, the program has extended across all five boroughs, equipping more than 1,400 buses and covering 560 miles of service, benefitting over 915,000 daily customers.

records.display_name: MTA ABLE (Automated Bus Lane Enforcement) Program

description: On-bus mobile camera system operated by MTA. Equipment installed inside buses captures rear license plates of vehicles stopped in bus lanes. Two buses must observe same vehicle at same GPS location at least 5 minutes apart to issue violation. Captures no standing and no parking violations.
Increased Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) mentioned as one of the initiatives being implemented to decrease wait time and travel time
Bus lane enforcement camera program. Benefits from pilot phase include speed improvements of 5% above borough average, collision reductions. 50K citations issued in 2022.
Expansion of Automated Bus Lane Enforcement cameras. 243 buses equipped on 11 routes across all five boroughs, aiming for 300 total buses

555 buses equipped with ABLE cameras on 19 routes across all five boroughs. Routes showed average speed improvements of over 5% and 20% reductions in collisions. Over 50% of ABLE routes run in equity priority areas impacting 450,000 average daily customers. Deploying to 20th route at end of the month.
Program to install camera systems on buses to enforce bus lane restrictions. Systems purchased, installed, operated, and maintained by vendors under contract with MTA/NYCT Department of Buses.
Program for purchase, installation, operation, and maintenance of ABLE systems to enforce bus lane traffic restrictions by issuing violation notices to vehicles obstructing bus lanes

Request for Board ratification of an immediate operating need and award of contracts to Hayden AI and Seon USA Corp for Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) systems, including purchase, installation, operation, and maintenance

Ratification of an Immediate Operating Need for contracts with Hayden AI and Seon USA Corp for purchase, installation, operation, and maintenance of Automated Bus Lane Enforcement systems for NYC Transit
Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) Phase 2A capital project for MTA Bus Company, budgeted at $1,312,500 with completion target of November 2022
Expansion of Automated Bus Lane Enforcement as a measure to improve bus service.
project_name: MTA ABLE (Automated Bus Lane Enforcement) Program
status: expanded - 300 buses and 9 new routes in 2022; 600 additional cameras planned by end of 2023
Ongoing - expansion and transition to ACE
